cards.text: The basic syntax for defining a user-defined function is: {{c1::returnType}} {{c2::functionName}}() { }
The function that initiates a function call is called the {{c1::caller}}. The function being called is the {{c2::callee}}.
The curly braces and statements in-between in a function definition are called the {{c1::function body}}.
To call a function, use the function's name followed by {{c1::parentheses}}, e.g., {{c2::functionName()}}

cards.question: What happens if you forget the parentheses when calling a function?
cards.code:
void doPrint() { }

int main() {
doPrint; // Missing parentheses!
return 0;
}
cards.answer: The program may not compile. If it does compile, the function will NOT be called.
cards.explanation: Forgetting the parentheses after the function name is a common mistake. The parentheses are required to actually call the function.

cards.question: What does this program output?
cards.explanation: Execution starts in main(), calls doPrint() which prints its message, then returns to main() to print the ending message.

cards.text: When a function call is made, the CPU "{{c1::puts a bookmark}}" at the current point of execution, executes the called function, then {{c2::returns}} to the bookmarked point.
Functions can be called {{c1::multiple times}}, and each call will execute the function body {{c2::independently}}.
C++ does {{c1::not}} support {{c2::nested functions}} (functions defined inside other functions).

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.code:
#include <iostream>

int main() {
void foo() { // Nested function
std::cout << "foo!\n";
}
foo();
return 0;
}
cards.answer: Illegal: Functions cannot be nested inside other functions in C++.
cards.explanation: C++ does not support nested functions. The function foo() must be defined outside of main() at global scope.

cards.concept: Why use functions instead of putting all code in main()
cards.explanation:
Functions provide a way to:
  • Split programs into small, modular chunks
  • Make code easier to organize and test
  • Reuse code without duplication
  • Make programs more manageable as they grow

cards.back: The function that initiates a function call (invokes another function)
